Advisor: Got it. Going back to that “clear plan” point—usually it starts with cash flow. What’s your gross income these days, roughly?
Client: It’s around 190 a year. Business income.
Advisor: Okay—around $190k gross. When you say business income, is it paid monthly or kind of lumpy?
Client: Monthly, mostly steady.
Advisor: And just to make sure I’m not mixing terms—when you say “around 190,” is that before taxes, like gross?

Advisor: Next piece: monthly spending. All-in, what do you think you spend per month—housing, food, kid stuff, everything?
Client: Like… eight grand? Maybe a little more.
Advisor: Eight-ish. If you had to pin it down—are we talking $8,500 a month, give or take?
Client: Yeah, $8,500 is about right.
Advisor: Okay. Now debt payments—credit cards, car loan, student loans, anything with a required monthly payment?

Client: Yeah, just me. Divorced, one kid, but I’m the only adult.
Advisor: Got it. Going back to that “clear plan” point—usually it starts with cash flow. What’s your gross income these days, roughly?
Client: It’s around 190 a year. Business income.
Advisor: Okay—around $190k gross. When you say business income, is it paid monthly or kind of lumpy?
Client: Monthly, mostly steady.

Client: Yeah, like a regular account. I always call it “the taxable one.”
Advisor: Got it. If we add everything investable together, do you know the more exact total? Even a statement total is fine.
Client: I pulled it up earlier—total investable is $532,350.
Advisor: Perfect—$532,350 investable assets total. Thanks.
Client: Yeah.

Client: Yeah, a small private markets thing—$42,650. Also shows up under the insurance company portal.
Advisor: Great, $42,650 private markets, insurance portal. And then your home—primary residence—what’s the value you’re using these days?
Client: About $391,300.
Advisor: Okay, $391,300 for the primary residence. Any mortgage or home equity loan against it?
Client: No mortgage. Paid off.

Client: Yep.
Advisor: Any other brokerage or bank accounts besides that big taxable one?
Client: There’s another one—$65,900. That one’s more like a brokerage.
Advisor: Okay, $65,900 in a brokerage-type account. And any private investments—private markets, partnerships, anything like that?

Advisor: Perfect—Other for residence state. Let me note that down—one sec.
Client: Okay.
Advisor: And then one more: the type of firm relationship—are we talking a U.S. RIA setup for you, like US_RIA, versus a bank channel or insurance-only channel?
Client: Yeah, US RIA. That’s what I’m looking for.
Advisor: Okay, US_RIA. Just to make sure I didn’t misunderstand—this isn’t through your bank branch or anything like that?

Client: Yeah, just me. Divorced, one kid, but I’m the only adult.

Client: Monthly, mostly steady.

Client: Hey Alex. Honestly, I just want a clear plan—like what to do next—without it turning into a whole ordeal.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. Quick basics first: it’s just you in the household, right—one adult?
Client: Yeah, just me. Divorced, one kid, but I’m the only adult.
Advisor: Got it. Going back to that “clear plan” point—usually it starts with cash flow. What’s your gross income these days, roughly?
Client: It’s around 190 a year. Business income.

Advisor: Hi Robert—good to meet you. I’m Alex. Before we get into the weeds, what would make this meeting a win for you today?
Client: Hey Alex. Honestly, I just want a clear plan—like what to do next—without it turning into a whole ordeal.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it practical. Quick basics first: it’s just you in the household, right—one adult?
Client: Yeah, just me. Divorced, one kid, but I’m the only adult.

Advisor: First one’s simple: are we planning this as U.S.-based—like country is the United States?
Client: Yes, U.S.
Advisor: Great. And then risk tolerance—when markets swing, do you see yourself as conservative, moderate, or aggressive?
Client: Probably moderate. I’m not trying to swing for the fences.
Advisor: Okay, moderate. When you say moderate—just to sanity-check—are you okay with some ups and downs as long as there’s a plan?

Client: Yeah, just me. Divorced, one kid, but I’m the only adult.
Advisor: Got it. Going back to that “clear plan” point—usually it starts with cash flow. What’s your gross income these days, roughly?
Client: It’s around 190 a year. Business income.
Advisor: Okay—around $190k gross. When you say business income, is it paid monthly or kind of lumpy?
Client: Monthly, mostly steady.

Advisor: Okay—35%. And is that based on last year’s return, or just a guess?
Client: More a guess. It feels high.
Advisor: Totally fair. If I said 32% instead of 35%, would that sound more like what you’ve seen on your return?
Client: Yeah—actually, yes. 32% is the one I remember seeing.
Advisor: Perfect—so we’ll use 32% as your bracket assumption.

Advisor: Got it. Going back to that “clear plan” point—usually it starts with cash flow. What’s your gross income these days, roughly?
Client: It’s around 190 a year. Business income.
Advisor: Okay—around $190k gross. When you say business income, is it paid monthly or kind of lumpy?
Client: Monthly, mostly steady.
Advisor: And just to make sure I’m not mixing terms—when you say “around 190,” is that before taxes, like gross?

Advisor: Great.
Advisor: Next—date of birth. I don’t need anything super personal beyond the date itself. What’s your DOB?
Client: January 1st, 1974.
Advisor: Jan 1, 1974—thank you.
Client: Yeah.

Advisor: Perfect—$191,650 gross. One sec—writing that down.
Client: Sure.
Advisor: And your work status right now—are you employed, self-employed, between jobs… how would you describe it?
Client: Employed. I’m working full-time.
Advisor: Employed, full-time—got it.

Advisor: Okay, $65,900 in a brokerage-type account. And any private investments—private markets, partnerships, anything like that?
Client: Yeah, a small private markets thing—$42,650. Also shows up under the insurance company portal.
Advisor: Great, $42,650 private markets, insurance portal. And then your home—primary residence—what’s the value you’re using these days?
Client: About $391,300.
Advisor: Okay, $391,300 for the primary residence. Any mortgage or home equity loan against it?

Advisor: Okay, Robert—before you jump, I just want to fill one small gap about you personally, then we’re basically done with the fact-find.
Client: Alright, yeah—go ahead.
Advisor: Earlier you said business income and you’re employed—when you think about your work, what bucket would you put it in? Like sales, operations, tech, that kind of thing.
Client: Sales. I’m in sales.
Advisor: Got it—sales. And is that like pure commission, or more of a base-plus-bonus setup?

Client: Yeah, just me. Divorced, one kid, but I’m the only adult.
Advisor: Got it. Going back to that “clear plan” point—usually it starts with cash flow. What’s your gross income these days, roughly?

Advisor: Quick admin thing—when we label the household, you’re the only adult in the household, right? Just you and your kid, but adult-wise it’s one.
Client: Yeah, one adult. Just me.
Advisor: Perfect. And I’m going to ask something that sounds a little weird: in our notes, we tag people as “Client 1,” “Client 2,” etc. Since it’s just you, you’re Client 1—does that sound right?
Client: Yeah, I’m Client 1.
